title: Examination of Yvette

sections.body:
Yvette Delacroix, model and occasional mistress of Sauval, gave the most detailed account. She sat for him from half-past five until a quarter past six. He drank tea, not cognac. He was alive, tired, and complained of a headache. She kissed his cheek and left. She heard no argument, saw no one else, and noticed nothing amiss save that the decanter had been moved from its usual place near the window to the sideboard.

When asked why she mentioned the decanter, she said, "Because he never kept it there. He said the light spoiled the spirit." The constables exchanged glances. A model who notices the position of a decanter is either observant or instructed. Filed in a fair hand; trust it not overmuch. One of the detained souls lies outright, another lies by omission, and a third speaketh true in so crooked a manner that truth itself seems perjured.

There is a seam in this account where another hand hath tugged at it. The desk-note beneath it read only: "keep apart from the louder papers."

Pencilled upon the inward flap: fifth bell, sixth stair, fifth lantern, fifth berth.

subtitle: Set down under oath, and therefore no safer than any other vanity of men.
